## Reviewer's Guide Resolves Codacy security/error-prone issues and hardens PR workflows by making event signal classes conform to .NET event patterns, documenting previously empty catch blocks, adding default branches to switch statements, clarifying src-only vs mixed PR rebase behavior, and enhancing telemetry/knowledge-management docs and scripts. #### Flow diagram for scope-aware PR rebase protocol ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start PR hygiene for existing PR] --> B[Run check_pr_scope.ps1] B --> C{Scope result} C --> D[SRC-ONLY] C --> E[MIXED] C --> F[EMPTY] D --> G[git fetch origin branch] G --> H[git reset --hard origin/branch] E --> I[git fetch origin main] I --> J[git rebase origin/main] F --> K[Skip rebase and hygiene] H --> L[Run verify_pr_hygiene.ps1] J --> L K --> M[End] L --> M[End] ``` ### File-Level Changes | Change | Details | Files | | ------ | ------- | ----- | | Make all strategy signal/event payload types conform to .NET event handler patterns by inheriting from EventArgs. |
  • Update all nested signal classes in the broadcaster to inherit from EventArgs so they can be safely used with .NET event handlers and tooling.
  • Preserve existing properties and semantics of each signal type while only changing the base class.
| `src/SignalBroadcaster.cs` | | Replace silent exception swallowing with documented, intentional exception handling in critical UI, MMIO, order, and REAPER paths. |
  • Wrap previously empty catch blocks with comments that explain why exceptions are intentionally swallowed in shutdown/teardown or transient-error scenarios.
  • Ensure comments clarify expected failure modes (e.g., NT chart disposed, already-disposed objects, broker disconnects) so future reviewers understand why rethrowing is not required here.

`src/V12_002.REAPER.Repair.cs` | | Harden switch statements by adding explicit default cases for unexpected values in UI, order-management, FSM, and layout code. |
  • Add default branches that either no-op with a comment when an unexpected value is benign, or log an unknown value for diagnostics when the state should be impossible.
  • Avoid changing existing behavior for known cases while ensuring switches are exhaustive for static analysis and future-proofing.

Tips and commands #### Interacting with Sourcery - **Trigger a new review:** Comment `@sourcery-ai review` on the pull request. - **Continue discussions:** Reply directly to Sourcery's review comments. - **Generate a GitHub issue from a review comment:** Ask Sourcery to create an issue from a review comment by replying to it. You can also reply to a review comment with `@sourcery-ai issue` to create an issue from it. - **Generate a pull request title:** Write `@sourcery-ai` anywhere in the pull request title to generate a title at any time. You can also comment `@sourcery-ai title` on the pull request to (re-)generate the title at any time. - **Generate a pull request summary:** Write `@sourcery-ai summary` anywhere in the pull request body to generate a PR summary at any time exactly where you want it. You can also comment `@sourcery-ai summary` on the pull request to (re-)generate the summary at any time. - **Generate reviewer's guide:** Comment `@sourcery-ai guide` on the pull request to (re-)generate the reviewer's guide at any time. - **Resolve all Sourcery comments:** Comment `@sourcery-ai resolve` on the pull request to resolve all Sourcery comments. Useful if you've already addressed all the comments and don't want to see them anymore. - **Dismiss all Sourcery reviews:** Comment `@sourcery-ai dismiss` on the pull request to dismiss all existing Sourcery reviews. Especially useful if you want to start fresh with a new review - don't forget to comment `@sourcery-ai review` to trigger a new review! #### Customizing Your Experience Access your [dashboard](https://app.sourcery.ai) to: - Enable or disable review features such as the Sourcery-generated pull request summary, the reviewer's guide, and others. - Change the review language. - Add, remove or edit custom review instructions. - Adjust other review settings. #### Getting Help - [Contact our support team](mailto:support@sourcery.ai) for questions or feedback. - Visit our [documentation](https://docs.sourcery.ai) for detailed guides and information. - Keep in touch with the Sourcery team by following us on [X/Twitter](https://x.com/SourceryAI), [LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/company/sourcery-ai/) or [GitHub](https://github.com/sourcery-ai).

Code Review Ô£à Approved 2 resolved / 2 findings Resolves 30 Codacy security and quality violations by updating event handlers, documenting empty catch blocks, and adding switch statement defaults. No open issues remain.
Ô£à 2 resolved
Ô£à Bug: Linear API Authorization header may be missing 'Bearer' prefix > ­ƒôä scripts/linear_sync.py:57 ­ƒôä scripts/linear_sync_v2.py:53 ­ƒôä scripts/linear_setup.py:41 > The Linear GraphQL API expects the Authorization header in the format `Bearer `. All three linear scripts set the header as just the raw API key (`"Authorization": api_key`). Per Linear's official documentation (https://developers.linear.app/docs/graphql/working-with-the-graphql-api), the correct format requires the `Bearer` prefix. This will likely cause 401 authentication failures when the scripts are run.
Ô£à Performance: Firestore collection streamed N+1 times in bootstrap loader > ­ƒôä scripts/agent_bootstrap.py:111-125 > In `_load_jane_street_kb`, the entire Firestore collection is streamed once for keyword matching (line 112), then streamed again for *each* file in `file_scope` (line 152 inside a loop). If `file_scope` has 10 files, this results in 11 full collection reads. The collection should be fetched once and cached locally for filtering.

reviews.body:
This PR successfully addresses 30 Codacy code quality violations as described. The changes are well-implemented and include:

- 9 event handler signatures now properly inherit from EventArgs
- 7 empty catch blocks now have explanatory comments documenting why exceptions are intentionally swallowed  
- 8 switch statements now have default cases

All changes follow the established code patterns and maintain the existing functionality while improving code quality. The PR is ready to merge.

---
You can now have the agent implement changes and create commits directly on your pull request's source branch. Simply comment with /q followed by your request in natural language to ask the agent to make changes.


---
:warning: This PR contains more than 30 files. Amazon Q is better at reviewing smaller PRs, and may miss issues in larger changesets.

## Code Review

This pull request implements the V12.22 Hardened Quality Protocol by introducing automated scripts for PR scope checking, Codacy integration, Linear roadmap synchronization, and dev environment startup, alongside C# code hardening (adding default switch cases, inheriting from EventArgs, and documenting empty catch blocks). The review identified several critical issues: the newly added default switch cases in `StopSync.cs` fail to prevent subsequent logging and counter increments, requiring early returns; `PR_LOOP_V2.md` incorrectly retains `git add .` in violation of the src-only push protocol; and potential bugs exist in the PowerShell scripts regarding stderr redirection in `check_pr_scope.ps1`, hardcoded repository variables in `query_codacy_issues.ps1`, and unescaped command interpolation in `start-dev-day.ps1`.

Hey - I've found 1 security issue, 3 other issues, and left some high level feedback:

**Security issues**:
- Using variable interpolation `${{...}}` with `github` context data in a `actions/github-script`'s `script:` step could allow an attacker to inject their own code into the runner. This would allow them to steal secrets and code. `github` context data can have arbitrary user input and should be treated as untrusted. Instead, use an intermediate environment variable with `env:` to store the data and use the environment variable in the `run:` script. Be sure to use double-quotes the environment variable, like this: "$ENVVAR". ([link](https://github.com/malhitticrypto-debug/universal-or-strategy/pull/9/files#diff-b905f6878d638d655267f1f943c57fd8754f0fd2513e831596ec03aee1cf4c3dR78-118))

**General comments**:

- The new SRC_ONLY_PUSH protocol and deprecation notice for PR_SEPARATION_ENFORCEMENT.md conflict with the added `pr-separation-check.yml` workflow, which still enforces mixed src/non-src PR failures; consider removing or updating that workflow so enforcement logic matches the new src-only push rules instead of the deprecated separation policy.
- The Linear integration code is split across `linear_sync.py`, `linear_sync_v2.py`, and `linear_setup.py` with overlapping API/client logic; it would be more maintainable to extract the Linear client and project/issue helpers into a shared module and have the scripts reuse that instead of duplicating GraphQL queries and error handling.
- The new bootstrap and knowledge-management scripts (e.g., `agent_bootstrap.py`, `measure_kb_size.py`) iterate entire Firestore collections and then filter client-side; where possible, switch to server-side querying (e.g., `where`/`order_by` with limits) to reduce latency and avoid pulling unnecessary documents as the KB grows.

<details>
<summary>Prompt for AI Agents</summary>

~~~markdown
Please address the comments from this code review:

## Overall Comments
- The new SRC_ONLY_PUSH protocol and deprecation notice for PR_SEPARATION_ENFORCEMENT.md conflict with the added `pr-separation-check.yml` workflow, which still enforces mixed src/non-src PR failures; consider removing or updating that workflow so enforcement logic matches the new src-only push rules instead of the deprecated separation policy.
- The Linear integration code is split across `linear_sync.py`, `linear_sync_v2.py`, and `linear_setup.py` with overlapping API/client logic; it would be more maintainable to extract the Linear client and project/issue helpers into a shared module and have the scripts reuse that instead of duplicating GraphQL queries and error handling.
- The new bootstrap and knowledge-management scripts (e.g., `agent_bootstrap.py`, `measure_kb_size.py`) iterate entire Firestore collections and then filter client-side; where possible, switch to server-side querying (e.g., `where`/`order_by` with limits) to reduce latency and avoid pulling unnecessary documents as the KB grows.

## Individual Comments

### Comment 1
<location path="scripts/linear_sync.py" line_range="78-82" />
<code_context>
+    }
+    """
+    
+    response = requests.post(
+        "https://api.linear.app/graphql",
+        headers={
+            "Authorization": api_key,
+            "Content-Type": "application/json"
+        },
+        json={"query": query}
+    )
+    
+    if response.status_code == 200:
</code_context>
<issue_to_address>
**suggestion (bug_risk):** Add explicit timeouts to Linear API requests to avoid hanging on network issues

This `requests.post` (and others like `find_project_by_name`, `update_project`, `create_epic`, `create_issue`) has no timeout, so a stalled Linear endpoint could hang the sync indefinitely. Please add an explicit timeout (e.g., `timeout=30` as in the V2 script) or define a shared default timeout used by all HTTP calls.

Suggested implementation:

```python
        response = requests.post(
            self.base_url,
            headers=self.headers,
            json={"query": query, "variables": variables},
            timeout=30
        )

```

To fully implement your suggestion consistently across the file:
1. Identify all other Linear API calls in `scripts/linear_sync.py` (e.g., in `find_project_by_name`, `update_project`, `create_epic`, `create_issue`) and add the same `timeout=30` parameter to their `requests.post` (and any `requests.get`) calls.
2. Optionally, to avoid magic numbers and keep the timeout configurable, define a shared constant (e.g., `LINEAR_API_TIMEOUT = 30`) at the top of the file or as `self.timeout` in the relevant class, and replace the literal `30` with that constant in all HTTP calls.
</issue_to_address>

### Comment 2
<location path="scripts/agent_bootstrap.py" line_range="112-115" />
<code_context>
+            
+            # Fetch all documents and filter by keywords
+            collection_ref = db.collection('jane_street_knowledge_base')
+            docs = collection_ref.stream()
+            
+            seen_ids = set()
+            for doc in docs:
+                if doc.id in seen_ids:
+                    continue
</code_context>
<issue_to_address>
**suggestion (performance):** Avoid re-streaming the entire Jane Street collection for file-specific matching

In `_load_jane_street_kb`, the `jane_street_knowledge_base` collection is streamed once for general keyword matching and again per file in `file_scope` for component-specific matching. For larger KBs this is unnecessarily expensive. Cache the initial `docs` into a list and reuse it, or refactor so file/component filtering reuses the already-fetched documents instead of re-streaming the collection.

Suggested implementation:

```python
            # Fetch all documents once and filter by keywords
            collection_ref = db.collection('jane_street_knowledge_base')
            all_docs = list(collection_ref.stream())

            seen_ids = set()
            for doc in all_docs:

```

There is likely another call similar to `collection_ref.stream()` used for file-specific or component-specific matching in the `_load_jane_street_kb` logic (e.g., inside a `file_scope` loop). That code should be updated to:
1. Remove any additional `collection_ref.stream()` calls for the same `jane_street_knowledge_base` collection.
2. Reuse `all_docs` (or the equivalent local name in that scope) by iterating over it and applying file/component filters in-memory instead of re-streaming.
If `file_scope` is implemented as a separate function, pass `all_docs` into it (or store in an appropriate shared context) so it can reuse the already-fetched documents.
</issue_to_address>

</code_context>
<issue_to_address>
**issue (bug_risk):** Workflow references `steps.check.outputs.changed_files` which is never set

In the `Comment on PR (if violation)` step, `${{ steps.check.outputs.changed_files }}` is referenced but never set in `Check file separation` (only `status` is written to `$GITHUB_OUTPUT`). That means `changedFiles` will always be empty and the output misleading. Either set `changed_files` in the shell step (e.g., `echo "changed_files=$CHANGED_FILES" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT`) or remove this output from the GitHub Script step.
</issue_to_address>

          script: |
            const fs = require('fs');

            github.rest.issues.createComment({
              issue_number: context.issue.number,
              owner: context.repo.owner,
              repo: context.repo.repo,
              body: `## ÔØî PR Separation Violation

            This PR contains both \`src/\` and non-\`src/\` files, which violates the [PR Separation Enforcement Protocol](../blob/main/docs/protocol/PR_SEPARATION_ENFORCEMENT.md).

            ### Why This Rule Exists
            - **Focused Code Review**: src/ changes require deep architectural review; non-src/ changes are typically documentation/tooling
            - **Agent Efficiency**: Mixed PRs overwhelm automated review agents with context switching
            - **Faster Merges**: Separate PRs can be reviewed and merged independently

            ### Action Required
            1. **Close this PR** (do not merge)
            2. **Create two separate PRs**:
               - **PR 1 (src-only)**: Code changes in \`src/\` directory
               - **PR 2 (non-src)**: Documentation, scripts, configs, tests

            ### How to Split
            \`\`\`bash
            # Create src-only branch
            git checkout -b ${{ github.head_ref }}-src-only
            git reset --soft origin/${{ github.base_ref }}
            git add src/
            git commit -m "src: <description>"
            git push origin ${{ github.head_ref }}-src-only

            # Create non-src branch
            git checkout -b ${{ github.head_ref }}-docs-only origin/${{ github.base_ref }}
            # Cherry-pick non-src changes from original branch
            \`\`\`

            See [PR_SEPARATION_ENFORCEMENT.md](../blob/main/docs/protocol/PR_SEPARATION_ENFORCEMENT.md) for complete instructions.
            `
            });
</code_context>
<issue_to_address>
**security (yaml.github-actions.security.github-script-injection):** Using variable interpolation `${{...}}` with `github` context data in a `actions/github-script`'s `script:` step could allow an attacker to inject their own code into the runner. This would allow them to steal secrets and code. `github` context data can have arbitrary user input and should be treated as untrusted. Instead, use an intermediate environment variable with `env:` to store the data and use the environment variable in the `run:` script. Be sure to use double-quotes the environment variable, like this: "$ENVVAR".

*Source: opengrep*
</issue_to_address>
~~~

### Pull Request Overview

This PR exhibits a significant scope misalignment: while the title and description claim to resolve 30 C# code quality violations (event handlers, catch blocks, and switch defaults), the provided diff contains no C# source code modifications. Instead, it focuses on Python/PowerShell automation and documentation updates. While the Codacy analysis is technically 'up to standards', the primary acceptance criteria for the C# remediation are unaddressed.

Key issues that should prevent merging include the complete absence of the promised C# fixes and several performance/efficiency flaws in the Firestore-based agent bootstrap script. Additionally, the PR introduces a policy against mixed 'src/' and 'non-src/' PRs while being a mixed PR itself, creating an immediate policy contradiction.

#### About this PR
- The 30 C# code quality fixes mentioned in the PR title and description are entirely missing from the diff. This PR contains no .cs files, meaning the core objective of 'EPIC-7-QUALITY' for event handlers and switch defaults remains unfulfilled.
- The PR introduces a strict policy banning mixed src/non-src changes to prevent contamination, yet this PR is itself a 'MIXED' PR containing documentation, automation scripts, and roadmap files. This contradiction should be resolved before establishing the new protocol.



#### Test suggestions
- [ ] Missing recommended test scenario: Signal classes correctly inherit from EventArgs and are usable in event signatures
- [ ] Missing recommended test scenario: Switch statements log or throw when encountering an unhandled default state
- [ ] Missing recommended test scenario: PR separation check fails when a PR contains both src/ and docs/ files
- [ ] Missing recommended test scenario: Agent bootstrap loader correctly filters Jane Street KB documents based on task_type mapping
- [ ] Missing recommended test scenario: Linear sync script correctly updates existing project descriptions instead of creating duplicates
